relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: After two full years, Pharaoh dreamed by the Nile.
Seven beautiful, full cows rose from the river.
Seven ugly, thin cows rose after them.
The thin consumed the full, and Pharaoh woke.
He dreamed again of seven full ears of grain.
Seven scorched, thin ears came after them.
The thin swallowed the full, and the dream troubled the king.
Egypt’s interpreters could not open the meaning.
The cupbearer finally remembered.
He recalled the prison.
He recalled the dreams.
He recalled the Hebrew youth who interpreted truly.
What Joseph spoke had happened.
Joseph was hurried from the pit, changed, and brought before Pharaoh.
Pharaoh asked for interpretation.
Joseph pointed beyond himself: the Divine will answer with peace.
Pharaoh recounted the river dream.
He saw the seven full cows.
He saw the seven wasted cows.
The wasted consumed the full.
Yet the wasted still looked wasted.
He recounted the full ears of grain.
He recounted the scorched ears.
The thin swallowed the good, and no magician could explain.
Joseph said the two dreams were one message from the Divine.
Seven good cows and seven good ears meant seven years.
The thin meant seven years of famine.
The Divine had shown Pharaoh what was coming.
Seven years of abundance would arrive.
Seven years of famine would follow and swallow memory of abundance.
The famine would be very heavy.
The doubled dream meant the matter was fixed and near.
Wisdom was needed to govern what was coming.
A fifth should be gathered during abundance.
Food should be stored under Pharaoh’s hand.
The reserve would keep the land alive during famine.
The word pleased Pharaoh and his servants.
They recognized the spirit of the Divine in Joseph.
Joseph was named discerning and wise.
He was placed over Pharaoh’s house and people.
Joseph was set over Egypt.
Signet, linen, and gold marked the reversal from pit to authority.
Joseph rode in honor through Egypt.
No hand or foot would move without Joseph.
Joseph received an Egyptian name, Asenath, and authority over the land.
At thirty, Joseph stood before Pharaoh and moved through Egypt.
Abundance poured from the earth.
Joseph gathered food into the cities.
Grain became beyond counting.
Before famine, Joseph’s sons were born.
Manasseh carried the name of forgetting hardship.
Ephraim carried fruitfulness in affliction.
The abundance ended.
Famine began, but Egypt had bread.
Pharaoh sent the hungry to Joseph.
Joseph opened the storehouses.
The earth came to Joseph for grain.
